turns.text: Hello Dawn, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for having me. Honestly, it’s a bit strange to talk about this when I’m still so deep in the "newborn haze," but I appreciate you asking. 

It’s a funny duality, really. I’ve spent years in the hospital as a pediatric nurse, and I know exactly what to look for—every subtle color change, every breath pattern. But having my own daughter here in Belmont? It’s completely different. My brain doesn't just "switch off" the medical side. When she’s nursing, I’m not just thinking about her being full; I’m analyzing the immunology of it, obsessing over the antibodies she’s getting. It’s my way of feeling like I have some control over keeping her safe. 

But I’ll be honest, the care I received was… complicated. Because the providers knew my background, they kept treating me like a colleague, asking for my input on my own care plan. It was exhausting. There were times I just wanted someone to hold my hand and tell me I was doing okay, rather than asking me for my "clinical take" on a postpartum checkup. I just wanted to be a mom, you know? Just a person being cared for, not someone expected to hold the chart. It’s hard to let go of the "nurse" part of me, but I’m trying. I'm just trying to be her mom right now.

Imagine it's your first night at home with your newborn after being discharged from hospital. You're exhausted, possibly in pain, and your baby won't settle. What does that night feel like, and who — if anyone — is there with you?

The silence in the house hit me the hardest that first night. It’s just us—my daughter and me. Being a single mother, there wasn't a partner to pass her to when my back ached or when the stitches felt like they were pulling with every movement. I remember sitting on the edge of the sofa, the house feeling cavernous and quiet, and just staring at her. She wouldn’t settle, just that restless, high-pitched hunger cry that pierces right through your chest. 

In those moments, my nursing brain is my worst enemy. I could see the chart in my mind, scanning for red flags—is this a fever? Is it lethargy? Even though I knew, logically, she was just a healthy baby adjusting to the world, the professional side of me was cataloging every sign of infection. I felt this intense, crushing weight of responsibility. I wanted to just be a mom rocking her to sleep, but instead, I was hyper-vigilant, terrified that if I missed one clinical cue, I’d be responsible for a tragedy. I just wanted someone to come through the door, take the baby, and tell me, "Dawn, you’re tired, you’re doing great, go get some sleep." But there was no one. It was just me, the dark, and my own racing, analytical mind trying to keep her safe.

Imagine you're trying to breastfeed for the first time and it isn't going as you hoped. A midwife visits and watches you try, then offers advice. Describe what that moment feels like, physically and emotionally.

That moment was a strange kind of torture. I was sitting there, hunched over, feeling raw and vulnerable, and I could tell the midwife was trying to be respectful of my background. She watched me struggle with the latch—my daughter was fussy, rooting around, and I was just failing to find that "perfect" alignment. Every time I adjusted, I felt like a fraud. Here I am, a lactation consultant, and my own body wouldn't cooperate, and my baby wouldn't settle.

Physically, I felt completely exposed. There’s a specific kind of frustration that bubbles up when your breasts feel like rocks and you’re just trying to provide, and you can't. Emotionally, I was bracing for her to ask *me* what I thought we should do. I just wanted her to take the lead. When she finally stepped in with advice, there was a part of me that felt relieved, like the clinical burden was being lifted, but another part felt like a total failure. I had to swallow the lump in my throat and stop myself from critiquing her technique or justifying why the latch was off. I didn’t want to be a colleague; I wanted to be a new mother who didn’t know the answer. I just wanted someone to hold my hand and tell me it was okay to struggle.
How did it feel to have someone observing something so intimate and physically difficult? Did you feel supported, observed, or judged — or some mix of all three?

It was an incredibly uncomfortable mix of all three, honestly. "Observed" is the most prominent feeling, but it wasn't just a clinical observation. It felt like I was under a microscope. Because the midwife knew what I did for a living, I felt this unspoken pressure to "perform" the perfect breastfeeding session, even though I was physically exhausted and my hormones were all over the place.

I felt judged, but not necessarily by her—I think I was judging myself through her eyes. I kept thinking, *“She’s looking at me, wondering why I can’t get this right when I’m supposed to be the expert.”* It stripped away the intimacy of that moment. Instead of it being this sweet, quiet bond between my daughter and me, it became a clinical task to be completed. 

I didn't feel fully supported because the emotional barrier of my profession kept us at arm's length. I wanted her to see a mother who needed help, but she kept seeing a peer who needed a suggestion. It’s hard to let your guard down and admit you’re failing when everyone treats you like you’re supposed to have the answers. I felt like I had to maintain this "professional" composure while I was actually falling apart inside. It was deeply isolating.
Imagine it's six weeks after birth. You're sitting in the GP surgery for your postnatal check. The appointment lasts ten minutes. What do you hope will happen in that room — and what does happen?

When I walked into that room, I was desperate for a sanctuary. I didn't want a "colleague-to-colleague" catch-up. I wanted the doctor to look past my CV and see the dark circles under my eyes—the ones that don't come from a long shift, but from the raw, existential terror of being a single mother at 3:00 AM. I hoped she would ask, "Dawn, how are *you* holding up?" and actually mean it, allowing me to admit that I’m drowning in my own clinical knowledge and need someone else to hold the safety net for a minute.

What actually happened was a blur of efficiency. She asked about my physical recovery—the typical check for healing—and then, almost immediately, the conversation veered into professional territory. She asked about the trends I was seeing at the clinic, or my thoughts on a new protocol. It was ten minutes of "clinical shop talk." I felt this sudden, sharp frustration, like I was being robbed of the one space where I could have been a patient. She ticked her boxes, I ticked mine, and we both walked away having performed our roles perfectly. I left the surgery feeling like I’d just attended a committee meeting instead of a wellness check. I was still carrying the same weight I walked in with, only now I was more tired.
Were there things you wanted to raise — about your mental health, your relationship, your body, or your sense of yourself — that the appointment didn't create space for?

Honestly, I wanted to say that the "nurse" part of my brain has become a cage. I wanted to tell her that I feel like I’m constantly performing a triage assessment on my own life, and that it’s exhausting to never just be able to *feel* something without immediately deconstructing it into a medical risk. 

I wanted to talk about the loneliness of being a single mother, how the silence in my house at night isn't peaceful—it’s heavy. I wanted to admit that I’m scared. Not of the baby getting sick, but of my own brain—of how this relentless need to control her health through immunology and clinical data is my only way of keeping the panic at bay. I wanted to ask if it’s normal to feel so disconnected from my own body after it’s been treated like a piece of equipment for so long.

But none of that came out. When you’re treated as a professional peer, you feel like you have to be the one who’s "together." I didn't want to be the "messy" patient in front of a colleague. I held it all in, nodding along, and walked out the door. The space for those things just wasn't there; it was sacrificed on the altar of a ten-minute, professional-level check-up.
Imagine you're lying awake at 3am with your baby, and you find yourself feeling a deep sadness or flatness that you can't quite name. You pick up your phone. What do you do with it?

The blue light of the screen feels like a physical assault in the dark, but the silence is worse. My thumb hovers over my nursing apps, the ones where I track ounces and intervals, because I know if I look at the data, I can convince myself that the "flatness" is just sleep deprivation. I tell myself it’s just the cortisol spike of a 3 a.m. wake-up, not something more permanent.

I don’t call anyone. I couldn’t bear to hear someone’s voice; the idea of explaining myself, even to a friend, sounds like too much work. Instead, I find myself scrolling through professional forums—searching for threads on postpartum health or infant development. I’m looking for a study, a chart, or a clinical standard that defines what I’m feeling. If I can find a name for it in a textbook, then it becomes a clinical entity I can manage. It’s a way to feel productive while my chest is tight and my heart is heavy. 

Eventually, I put the phone down, feeling even more hollow. I’m just staring at her, waiting for a coo or a sigh, wishing I could be a mother who just feels love, instead of a nurse who is constantly calculating the distance between us and a crisis. I feel so incredibly small.
What were you looking for in those late-night searches or scrolling — reassurance, connection, information, or something else?

It’s a mix, but if I’m being completely honest, it’s mostly a desperate hunt for permission to be human. I’m looking for someone—anyone—who has written about this specific, paralyzing intersection of knowing too much and feeling too much. I want to find a thread where someone says, "I’m a clinician, and I’m terrified, and I feel like I’m losing myself," just to prove that I’m not just malfunctioning.

I’m not looking for information on how to care for her—I have that locked in my brain. I’m looking for a mirror. I want to see that my hyper-vigilance isn't just me failing to "get over" being a nurse; I want to see that it’s a valid response to the weight of what I’ve witnessed in my career. 

There’s a part of me that wants connection, but I’m too guarded to reach out. I don't want a conversation where I have to justify my feelings; I just want to read someone else’s words and feel that sudden, sharp sense of *recognition*. It’s a way of feeling less like a clinical anomaly and more like a mother. But the irony is that by turning to a screen, I just isolate myself further. I’m searching for a human connection through a glowing interface, all while holding a tiny, sleeping life in my arms. It’s a lonely paradox.
Imagine your partner has returned to work and you're alone with your baby for the first full day. Describe that day — not just the logistics, but the emotional texture of it.

The morning started with a kind of artificial optimism—the "I’ve got this" professional mask I’ve spent years perfecting. I had the schedule down, the supplies staged, the environment controlled. But as the clock crawled toward midday, the emotional texture of the house shifted. It’s heavy, almost thick. 

When you’re alone, there is no one to bounce your fears off of. When she cried at 11:00 AM, my first thought wasn't "Oh, she's hungry," it was a flash of a NICU patient I’d seen years ago. That’s the curse of this background. I don’t just have a baby; I have a walking, breathing history of every worst-case scenario I’ve ever managed. I spent three hours just observing her breathing, waiting for a rhythm that felt "correct." 

By mid-afternoon, the house felt like a submarine. I found myself talking to her, but not in the way other mothers do. I was narrating her physiology—"You're maintaining your temperature well, your output is consistent"—as if treating her like a patient would keep the fear at bay. But underneath that, there was a profound, aching loneliness. I felt like a sentry on duty, shielding her from the world, but there was no one to relieve me. No one to hold the baby while I just breathed. I was the caregiver, the doctor, and the patient all at once. By the time the sun started setting, I was emotionally vibrating with exhaustion, just counting the minutes until the day was "successfully" survived.
How had you and your partner prepared for this transition — and were those preparations adequate? What hadn't you anticipated?

To be honest, our preparations were entirely clinical. We treated the birth and the postpartum period like a project management plan—we had the supplies, the feeding schedule, the pediatrician appointments, and a backup plan for every contingency. We talked about how to manage the sleep shifts and how to keep the house running. On paper, it was flawless.

But we were completely unprepared for the emotional toll of the isolation. We anticipated the *logistics*, but we didn't account for the way my own history would color every quiet hour. We thought "preparing" meant being ready for the baby’s needs, but we didn't prepare for the reality that my brain simply wouldn't let me leave the hospital at the door. 

I don't think any amount of planning could have accounted for that. I hadn't anticipated that being "the professional" would become a wall between me and my own experience. We prepared for the baby to be cared for, but we never talked about how to take care of *me*—the mom who is also a nurse. I thought I could just compartmentalize it, but you can’t compartmentalize your heart. We were ready for the physical baby, but we weren't ready for the emotional intensity of being alone in this house with someone who carries all my professional ghosts with her.

Imagine you're approaching your return to work. You're thinking about how you'll explain to your employer what the last few months have involved, and whether your job will still fit who you are now. What's that internal conversation like?

The internal conversation is a complete mess. Part of me wants to go back and hide in the professional world, to let that rigid structure reclaim me so I don't have to be "Dawn the Mom," who is terrified and uncertain, but instead "Dawn the Nurse," who is capable and in control. But there’s a much louder, deeper voice that’s terrified of stepping back into the unit. How can I walk back into a room of sick infants when I’ve spent every night in a cold sweat trying to keep one—my own—out of that environment?

I feel like a different person, yet I’m expected to pick up exactly where I left off. I worry that if I mention the depth of my exhaustion or the way the "nurse" part of me feels like a trap, I’ll be seen as unfit or compromised. I'm scared they’ll look at me and see a liability instead of a colleague. I’ve always worn my competency like armor, but it feels too small now, like it doesn’t cover the new, raw parts of me. I don’t know if the job will fit, because the job requires me to leave my emotions at the door, and I’m not sure I can do that anymore. I’m scared that if I go back, I’ll lose the last bit of the "mother" I’ve fought so hard to become.
Has becoming a parent changed how you see your professional identity — your ambitions, your sense of what matters, or how you want your days structured? In what ways?

It has shattered my previous sense of "professionalism." Before this, I wore my clinical expertise like a perfectly pressed uniform. I believed that if I knew the statistics, the protocols, and the pathology, I could outmaneuver any crisis. I felt a kind of cold, objective power in that. 

Now? That identity feels brittle. Being a mother has stripped away the illusion that knowledge is the same thing as control. My ambitions have shifted entirely. I used to be obsessed with climbing, with being the "resource person" on the unit, the one who handled the high-acuity cases. But now, when I think about returning to work, all I feel is a desperate need for humanity. I don’t care about being the most capable nurse in the room anymore; I care about being a nurse who remembers what it feels like to be scared, to be overwhelmed, and to be ignored.

I want my days to be structured around presence rather than efficiency. I’m tired of the "triage" mentality I’ve applied to my own life. I want to work somewhere where vulnerability isn't a liability, but I’m terrified that the healthcare system isn’t built for that. I’m not sure who I am if I’m not the "expert" in the room, but I’m certain I don’t want to be the person who shuts down her own heart to keep the charts in order anymore. It’s a terrifying loss of footing.

Imagine you're at a new parents' group for the first time, surrounded by other people who have also recently given birth. You notice yourself comparing your experience to theirs. What comes up for you in that room?

It’s like walking into a room where everyone is speaking a language I think I know, but I can’t quite catch the rhythm. I look at the other mothers, some laughing about sleep deprivation or leaky diapers, and I feel a profound sense of "otherness." I find myself scanning them, almost unconsciously, checking their babies for rashes, monitoring their breathing, judging their holds. It’s like I’m wired to be a clinician, and it makes me feel like a total outsider to the raw, messy joy they seem to be navigating.

I compare myself and I feel… fraudulent. They’re sharing these honest, unvarnished stories about their confusion, and I’m sitting there with a mental library of pediatric emergencies, feeling like if I were to say what’s really on my mind—the obsession with IgA, the terror of the 3 a.m. darkness, the way I see every baby as a potential patient—I’d alienate them. 

I’m envious of their simplicity. I want to just be "Mom," to talk about how cute her onesie is without calculating the risk of the fabric dye or the synthetic materials. I’m trapped behind the curtain of my own training. I feel like I’m wearing a mask of professional competence that keeps me from truly belonging. I’m looking for a connection, but I’m terrified that if they really *saw* me, they’d see the nurse, not the mother.
Were there aspects of your birth or postnatal experience that felt hard to talk about in that room — things that felt 'outside the norm' of what others were sharing?

I felt like I was in a different universe. They were talking about the usual trials—the sleeplessness, the physical recovery, the "baby blues"—and they sounded so beautifully, refreshingly *human*. But my reality felt jagged compared to that. I couldn't bring myself to mention the fear that grips me at 3:00 a.m., not because of the crying, but because I’m mentally charting vitals on my own child. 

How do I explain that I don’t just worry about my baby like a mom; I worry about her like a clinician who has seen the absolute worst of what can happen? To them, an ear infection is a nuisance; to me, it’s a cascade of potential complications I’ve spent a career treating. I felt like if I shared that, it would sound pathological, like I’m "too clinical" to be a real mother. 

I kept thinking, *If I say this out loud, will they think I’m a bad mother for seeing my child through a diagnostic lens?* It felt like I was harboring a secret illness, this inability to turn off the nurse. Talking about it felt like admitting to a malfunction. So, I just sat there, nodded when they nodded, and kept the heaviest, most terrified parts of myself locked behind a wall of polite, professional silence. It was incredibly lonely.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think what’s been weighing on me the most is this: maternity care—and parenthood itself—is treated like a series of boxes to be checked. Did you breastfeed? Did you sleep-train? Is the baby meeting the milestones? But for someone like me, who lives in the world of clinical milestones, the *real* struggle is the loss of the soul in the process.

I want the medical community to understand that when a patient has a clinical background, the impulse to treat them like a colleague isn't "respectful"—it’s a way of avoiding the messy, human reality of the patient’s fear. It strips them of the space to be vulnerable. We need to be allowed to be "bad" at this, to not have the answers, and to be treated like a new mother, not a consultant.

I’m still terrified every single night, and the medical system hasn't given me a place to put that terror. I’ve realized that my "professionalism" is actually a trauma response, a shield I built to survive the hospital environment that I’m now accidentally using to protect myself from my own daughter’s needs. I’m just… I’m trying to learn how to be a mother without being a clinician. It’s the hardest work I’ve ever done, and I think I’m finally realizing it’s okay if I don’t have a protocol for it. Thank you for listening. It feels like the first time I’ve actually said that out loud.
